title: The production of episodes 25 and 26
wikitext:
[]: The production of episodes 25 and 26             Quote Post by CitizenGeek » Thu Sep 13, 2007 1:50 pm I'm not 100% certain about what caused such a drastic change in ... pretty much everything in the final two eps of Eva. The popular theory is that Gainax ran out of money. I don't buy this, to be honest! An Irish journalist called Rob Fahey who seems very connected to the world of Japanese animation (In fact, he worked for Gonzo for a while and has been to Japan on many occasions) wrote about Evangelion on Eurogamer.net (in the anime fan group). Unforetunately, this group cannot be accessed unless you are a member. I'll just quote what he wrote: The running out of cash thing isn't true - that's something that's become a popular theory more recently, which confuses me a bit because at the time, there was absolutely no debate over what happened to the final episodes. In fact, it was reported in Japanese newspapers at the time - it was quite a big news story, given Eva's huge popularity in its original terrestrial broadcasts. Anno and the rest of the Gainax team had been pushing production of the various episodes up closer and closer to the deadlines issued by their TV station, to the extent that they were actually biking episodes over to the broadcast centre on Beta tape only minutes before the broadcast time. This was tolerated, because Eva had some of the highest ratings of any show ever - until Episode 24. Episode 24 arrived at the station so late that it couldn't even be watched before being broadcast - it was slapped in a Beta drive and transmitted directly, without being reviewed by any staff at the station. Episode 24, you'll recall, is the Kaworu episode, which aside from the homosexual overtones (probably not actually an issue), ends with, er, a pop. Bear in mind that this was broadcast in a 6.30pm weekday slot on Japanese national television. The network went apeshit - it may have been in silhouette, and he may have been technically non-human, but you can't show a 14 year old popping his 14 year old friend's head off at 6.30pm on TV, even in Japan. Gainax, and Anno specifically, were accused of deliberately holding back the episode to prevent the network from seeing it before broadcast - and the network staff demanded to see the storyboards for the next two episodes. Since the storyboards they were then shown were the two episodes which ended up in EoE, it's no surprise that the network execs told Gainax that they were having a bleedin' bubble, and insisted (with seven days to go!) that they make new episodes instead. Hence the original episode 25 and 26; all re-used footage, with a few random bits and bobs of footage that had been animated for other purposes or cut from earlier episodes, and a few frames of new footage. 25 and 26 were the "fuck you" episodes, absolute nonsense thrown together on an incredibly short schedule after they were told that the original planned episodes wouldn't be screened. (I think that the theories about Gainax running out of cash may be down to people getting confused over some other facts about the studio - Gainax DID run into major financial trouble, but this was several years after Eva was released. In fact, the trouble was down to the fact that they hadn't paid their taxes on a lot of the money they made from Eva, and the fines levied by the government as a result almost bankrupted the company...)

[]:             Quote Post by slothen » Thu Sep 13, 2007 3:17 pm [URL=http://img338.imageshack.us/my.php?image=1186386136037vc9.jpg] [/URL] This from the same country that brought us Battle Royale? Of course, its the same story as here in the US.... show that pushes the envelope is wildly popular, then takes one step over the edge and society's collective conscience kicks into gear to condemn it. Well, thats not entirely true. I was under the impression that Gainax was at the center of a shit-storm ever since the "do you want to become one with me" scene. I suppose due to certain contracts, Gainax couldn't just say "fuck you!" and not make new episodes? Maybe it would have been better if the remade 25-26 never aired.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Sep 13, 2007 3:42 pm I don't know about the rest, but this part: 25 and 26 were the "fuck you" episodes, absolute nonsense thrown together on an incredibly short schedule after they were told that the original planned episodes wouldn't be screened.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Thu Sep 13, 2007 3:46 pm Waitwaitwait. They were planning to have all of EoE in just two regular-length episodes? They were definitely running out of budget. That's pretty obvious from the number of lengthy still shots later in the series, especially in episode 24. How could they have possibly animated all that happened in EoE? The MP battle couldn't have occurred nearly the same way as it did in the movies. Forget Komm Susser Tod and the splat scenes, all the dots swirling... no way that could have happened.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Sep 13, 2007 3:49 pm One of those Eva special features, I forget which one (maybe that Genesis 00 thing), has animatics for the originally planned Episode 25 that have nearly or EXACT same scenes as what we see in Movie Episode 25', for instance, Misato giving her farewell speech to Shinji. Not that I've seen that special myself, just based on what I've heard from other fans. And I know for a fact I heard somewhere that Gainax was going to make an Eva movie regardless of the ending; probably on ANF or some such thing.

[]:             Quote Post by slothen » Thu Sep 13, 2007 3:58 pm considering how much time in EoE is wasted on unnecessary bullshit such as the live action sequences and Shinji's goddamn sandcastle scene, it could be done. And by "could be done," i mean it would have been possible to tell the story told in EoE in the space of two episodes. Of course lots of sections would have to have been shortened or some scenes removed. Now, considering the state of both funding and production, it probably would not have been done very well.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Thu Sep 13, 2007 3:59 pm Yes, I've seen a few boards from that... place... the one that has the dismembered arm ending... but I had assumed they were made before the budget went all bye-bye. Then again, maybe they couldn't have, with the hectic schedule being hectic. Now I want to know what EoE would have been like with a low budget. Several sequences would definitely be different or gone.

[]:             Quote Post by Szmitten » Thu Sep 13, 2007 4:19 pm This sounds about right to be honest. We already know that production of the movies started BEFORE the final two episodes aired; what other explanation for that could there be if it was already storyboarded? Oh wait, we already knew it was.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Sep 13, 2007 4:56 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: One of those Eva special features, I forget which one (maybe that Genesis 00 thing), has animatics for the originally planned Episode 25.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Thu Sep 13, 2007 8:25 pm One question remains: why the crap didn't we get these storyboards with the Instrumental Extras? :o

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Thu Sep 13, 2007 9:07 pm NAveryW wrote: One question remains: why the crap didn't we get these storyboards with the Instrumental Extras? :o

[]:             Quote Post by EvaCub » Fri Sep 14, 2007 10:26 am BrikHaus wrote: NAveryW wrote: One question remains: why the crap didn't we get these storyboards with the Instrumental Extras? :o

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Fri Sep 14, 2007 7:23 pm Yeah, it's still seeding. Get it while you can!

[]:             Quote Post by EvaCub » Fri Sep 14, 2007 8:46 pm BrikHaus wrote: Yeah, it's still seeding. Get it while you can!

[]:             Quote Post by CitizenGeek » Sat Sep 15, 2007 5:17 am Sailor Star Dust wrote: I don't know about the rest, but this part: 25 and 26 were the "fuck you" episodes, absolute nonsense thrown together on an incredibly short schedule after they were told that the original planned episodes wouldn't be screened.

[]:             Quote Post by BLACKANGEL32076 » Mon Sep 17, 2007 12:41 pm *off topic* I just thought of something. Why in God's name did this series have such a hard time keeping a schedule? Did money have anything to do with it, or was Anno and the productions team just lazy as sin? This has never been explained to me to any real degree. *back on topic*

[]:             Quote Post by pen^2 » Tue Sep 18, 2007 6:03 pm BLACKANGEL32076 wrote: I just thought of something. Why in God's name did this series have such a hard time keeping a schedule? Did money have anything to do with it, or was Anno and the productions team just lazy as sin? This has never been explained to me to any real degree.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Tue Sep 18, 2007 6:17 pm ....Maybe both??

[]:             Quote Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Tue Sep 18, 2007 6:28 pm I think Anno has an international patent on this damn process. :roll:

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Tue Sep 18, 2007 7:01 pm I think the process was fine, maybe the animation was simply too ambitious. Without the aid of animating and using a computer to do the inking, they simply had too many cells to transfer, ink, and photograph and ended up behind schedule (these shows are produced as they are aired, with a week or so of leeway). The number of cells used just for the explosions near the beginning of the show had to be quite a few. I can't remember where I first read that there were budget (management/planning) problems aside from the sponsors, but it doesn't sound implausible.
